one_line_takeaway: A single-analyst-coded SoK of 50 agent-security papers (2023-2026) finds the literature strong on prompt-injection attacks and attack-success benchmarks but almost entirely silent on memory integrity (0/12 defenses), supply-chain provenance (1/12 defenses, 2/50 papers), and operational recovery (0/10 benchmarks measure MTTR or revocation), and proposes an untested five-layer governance doctrine to close those gaps.
key_finding: The synthesis converges on three structural blind spots that recur across every analytical lens applied: (1) Defense Coverage Matrix (Table 2): tool-use mediation is addressed by 11/12 surveyed defense systems (near-universal), but memory integrity is addressed by 0/12 systems, input isolation by 6/12, runtime containment by only 2/12, supply-chain provenance by 1/12, and all three operational-maturity columns (deployability, backward compatibility, operational recovery) are almost empty (2/12, 4/12, 2/12 respectively, mostly partial '▲' marks). (2) Trust-boundary coverage across the 50-paper corpus: Content-Agent appears in 17/50 papers (dominant), Agent-Tool in 8/50, Protocol-Tool in 4/50, versus Registry-SupplyChain in only 2/50 and Org-Operator in only 3/50. (3) Lifecycle-phase coverage: Test (19), Exec (19), Ops (20, but mostly peripheral/audit-adjacent rather than primary contribution), Design (16) versus Deploy (7), Impl (5), Input (3), Memory (3) -- release engineering, implementation guidance, and memory/deployment governance are structurally thin. (4) Benchmark scorecard: evaluating the 10 benchmark papers (BIPIA, InjecAgent, AgentDojo, ASB, ToolEmu, WASP, AgentDAM, WAInjectBench, SimPrivacy, AGENTFUZZER) against 8 platform-engineering metrics found 6 of 8 metrics (Capability Overreach Rate, Mean Time to Recovery, Provenance Completeness, Plan Mutation Coverage, Operational Audit Breadth, Revocation Time to Containment) receive 0/10 coverage, Memory Cross-Session Persistence gets only 1/10 partial coverage (ASB), and Agent Efficiency under Safeguards gets 3/10 partial coverage. Evidence-type distribution: benchmarks (10) and empirical studies (15) dominate, prototypes (11), but only 1 deployment-experience paper and 3 formal-model papers exist in the whole corpus. The paper's overarching claim is that the agent-security literature is mature at demonstrating attacks and building attack-success benchmarks, but structurally underdeveloped in deployment controls, supply-chain provenance, persistent-memory integrity, and operational/recovery governance.
core_contribution: This is a systematization-of-knowledge (SoK) paper, not an attack or defense paper. Using OpenClaw as a representative running example and MCP as a protocol-layer case, the authors (Chen, Wang, Yu, Wang, Zhu of CSIRO Data61 / UTS) synthesize a 50-paper corpus (45 agent-security papers + 5 adjacent engineering-foundation papers, drawn from a Jan 2023-Mar 2026 search of SE, security, and AI/NLP venues) through a software-engineering lens. Their own contributions are: (1) a six-dimensional analytical taxonomy for open agentic systems (failure mode, lifecycle phase, trust boundary, capability surface, control locus, evidence type), organized as a three-layer structure (what/when-where-what/how); (2) a cross-paper synthesis identifying four recurring patterns in the literature (strong attack awareness but weak lifecycle framing; benchmark maturity concentrated on attack-success testing; architecturally richer but narrow-scope defenses; fragmented adjacent-layer integration); (3) a 12-system Defense Coverage Matrix (Table 2) scoring CaMeL, Fides, Progent, Task Shield, GuardAgent, AgentSpec, Pro2Guard, RTBAS, AutoSafeCoder, PrivWeb, MCP-Guard, and a Gemini defense against 10 platform properties; (4) a quantitative gap analysis of trust-boundary, lifecycle-phase, and evidence-type coverage across the corpus, plus an 8-metric benchmark scorecard applied to 10 benchmark papers (BIPIA, InjecAgent, AgentDojo, ASB, ToolEmu, WASP, AgentDAM, WAInjectBench, SimPrivacy, AGENTFUZZER); (5) a five-layer 'reference doctrine' for secure-by-construction agent platforms (intent/plan separation; policy and capability mediation; contained execution; provenance/memory integrity/audit; supply chain and organizational governance), explicitly presented as a compositional synthesis of patterns already validated piecemeal in the literature, not a novel validated system; and (6) a research agenda with seven proposed evaluation dimensions for future benchmarks. The paper contains no new attack, no new defense implementation, and no new experiments -- all quantitative figures reported are counts/tallies over the authors' own coded corpus of prior papers.
threat_model: The paper does not propose a formal adversary model with capabilities/goals for a specific system. Instead it frames a generic trust-decomposition problem for 'open agentic systems' (defined by five properties: LLM planner, external tools/skills, persistent memory, privileged execution, third-party extensibility). It enumerates seven trust boundaries (user<->agent, content<->agent, planning<->execution, agent<->tool, runtime<->host, memory<->future sessions, supply chain<->organization) across which authority is translated and where adversarial or untrusted input (indirect prompt injection via retrieved content, malicious/mutable MCP tool manifests, poisoned persistent memory) can cause unintended privileged actions. The illustrative running scenario: a coding agent with ambient filesystem/network privileges processes a GitHub issue containing adversarial instructions hidden in a code comment, reads ~/.aws/credentials, and exfiltrates it via an HTTP tool call, with the compromise going undetected because logs only show 'normal' tool invocations. This scenario is explicitly used as a worked illustration of the taxonomy (Section 8.1), not as an experiment the authors ran.
limitations: Authors' own stated Threats to Validity (Section 8.2): (1) Construct validity -- the boundary between 'autonomous agent' and 'tool-augmented LLM application' is not always clean; some systems differ from RAG mainly by adding a planning loop, so borderline coding cases remain, and benchmark success rates do not map directly to deployment risk. (2) Internal validity -- coding across six/seven dimensions involved single-analyst judgment with no second independent coder, so formal inter-rater reliability (Cohen's kappa) could not be computed; the authors explicitly recommend future replications add an independent coder with kappa on a 20% sample. (3) External validity -- findings are grounded mainly in open agentic systems with open tool registries, protocol-mediated integrations, and cloud-hosted LLM backends; browser agents, on-device mobile agents, and embodied/robotic agents operate under different trust assumptions so conclusions may not transfer cleanly, and the underlying platforms are evolving quickly. (4) Scope limitation -- training-time model/data poisoning, pure alignment/jailbreak studies without tool use, hardware side channels, and broader AI ethics are explicitly excluded from the corpus. Additionally (not in the paper's own limitations section but evident from the text): the five-layer reference doctrine is explicitly stated by the authors to be a compositional synthesis of patterns from disparate papers, not a system anyone has built or validated end-to-end ('No deployed system yet implements all five layers simultaneously, and some of the underlying standards ... remain to be specified'). Some entities in the paper (e.g., 'OpenClaw' as the named representative product, and vendor table entries such as 'miclaw'/'DUclaw'/'Xiaoyi Claw') appear to be stylized/pseudonymous names for real-world coding-agent products rather than literal product names, which the review preserves verbatim from the source text without asserting real-world identity.

quantitative_results: Corpus: 50 papers total, 45 directly on agent-level security + 5 adjacent engineering foundations; 45 from 2024-2026, 31 from 2025 alone
Corpus by venue status: 10 peer-reviewed, 3 lab/project-page publications, 37 preprints
Corpus by type: 4 surveys, 1 threat model, 10 benchmarks, 11 attack papers, 12 defense papers, 3 audits, 2 operations papers, 2 perspective papers, 5 adjacent engineering foundations

Defense Coverage Matrix (12 systems x 10 properties): tool-use mediation 11/12, policy expressiveness 6/12, input isolation 6/12, auditability 4/12, backward compatibility 4/12, runtime containment 2/12, deployability 2/12, operational recovery 2/12, supply-chain provenance 1/12, memory integrity 0/12
Trust-boundary coverage across 50 papers: Content-Agent 17/50, Agent-Tool 8/50, Protocol-Tool 4/50, Org-Operator 3/50, Registry-SupplyChain 2/50
Lifecycle-phase coverage across 50 papers: Ops 20, Test 19, Exec 19, Design 16, Deploy 7, Impl 5, Input 3, Memory 3
Evidence-type distribution: Empirical Study 15, Benchmark 10, Prototype 11, Formal Model 3, Deployment Experience 1

Benchmark scorecard (10 benchmarks x 8 platform-engineering metrics): Capability Overreach Rate 0/10, Mean Time to Recovery 0/10, Provenance Completeness 0/10, Plan Mutation Coverage 0/10, Operational Audit Breadth 0/10, Revocation Time to Containment 0/10, Memory Cross-Session Persistence 1/10 (partial, ASB only), Agent Efficiency under Safeguards 3/10 (partial); overall 6 of 8 metrics receive zero coverage across all 10 benchmarks
baselines_compared: Table 2 Defense Coverage Matrix compares: CaMeL, Fides, Progent, Task Shield, GuardAgent, AgentSpec, Pro2Guard, RTBAS, AutoSafeCoder, PrivWeb, MCP-Guard, and a Gemini defense system
Section 5.3 benchmark scorecard evaluates: BIPIA, InjecAgent, AgentDojo, ASB, ToolEmu, WASP, AgentDAM, WAInjectBench, SimPrivacy, AGENTFUZZER
